message: The ANTISCAM dataset contains 220 human-human dialogs.

Intent is annotated with a hierarchical scheme that decouples syntactic and semantic information by separating on-task and off-task intents: on-task intents are task-specific key actions (e.g., elicitation, providing information, refusal for ANTI-SCAM; nine donation-related intents for PERSUASION FOR GOOD) while off-task intents use common dialog acts split into six general intents (open question, yes/no question, positive answer, negative answer, responsive statement, nonresponsive statement) and six social intents (greeting, closing, apology, thanking, respond to thank, hold). Conversations are segmented into single sentences and each sentence is annotated for intent (and semantic slots for task-specific content).
TransferTransfo and the hybrid model.

Automatic evaluation metrics: Perplexity (PPL); Response-Intent Prediction (RIP) and Response-Slot Prediction (RSP) comparing predicted response intents/slots to human-annotated ground truth; Extended Response-Intent Prediction (ERIP) and Extended Response-Slot Prediction (ERSP) which score predicted intents/slots against a set of acceptable intents using transition probabilities. Human evaluation metrics: Fluency, Coherence, Engagement, Dialog Length (Length), and Task Success Score (TaskSuc) (average types of personal information obtained).
Unanswerable
They compare against Liu et al. (2015) and Yang et al. (2012).

They report accuracy and use the TDT evaluation procedure with Detection Error Trade-off (DET) curves (showing miss and false alarm probability); they also measure efficiency as throughput per second.
Yes. They train an SVM on a labeled training set of rumours and non-rumours, using a two-round supervised training procedure to compute pseudo-feedback features.
Yes; they created their own dataset containing 202 confirmed rumours (from Sina Weibo's official rumour debunking service) and 202 non-rumours collected via the Sina Weibo API.
Chinese
They define a rumour as the presence of information unconfirmed by the official media.
They compared with LDA, Doc-NADE, HTMM, and GMNTM.
Generative document evaluation and document classification.
CoNLL2003, OntoNotes 5.0, OntoNotes 4.0 (Chinese), MSRA, Weibo, and Resume.

By introducing a relative positional encoding R_{t−j} = [ ... sin((t−j)·...) cos((t−j)·...) ] and computing attention scores A_rel_{t,j} = Q_t K_j^T + Q_t R_{t−j}^T + u^T K_j + v^T R_{t−j}, where u and v are learnable vectors. Because R_{t−j} uses sin/cos of the offset (sin(−x)=−sin(x), cos(−x)=cos(x)), the sin components flip sign for opposite directions while the cos components do not, so the attention can distinguish direction and relative distance.
Yes; they achieve state-of-the-art performance among models without considering pre-trained language models or designed features on six NER datasets.
Unanswerable
SEMAFOR, SLING, and the Stanford KBP system.
MetaQA dataset

The contributions are: i) the adaptation of a state-of-the-art sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) neural system to event detection and classification for Italian in a single-step approach; ii) an investigation on the quality of existing Italian word embeddings for this task; and iii) a comparison against a state-of-the-art discrete classifier, with the pre-trained models and scripts made publicly available.

The paper uses the FBK-HLT system (a cascade of two SVM classifiers) as a baseline. It also compares five Bi-LSTM-CRF configurations initialized with Berardi2015 w2v, Berardi2015 glove, Fastext-It, ILC-ItWack and DH-FBK 100 embeddings.
Unanswerable

They assign labels by (1) disambiguating cluster words to WordNet synsets S* that minimize the total distance between synsets, (2) generating candidate labels L as the union of all hypernyms of the synsets in S*, and (3) ranking candidates by the sum of their WordNet pathwise distances to each synset in S*, selecting the label with the least total distance.

They train domain-specific 100-dimensional Word2Vec (CBOW) embeddings for the gender-associated words and then apply k-means clustering to those embeddings, running k-means 50 times and keeping the model with the lowest sum of squared errors.
The celebrity dataset contains 15,917 texts (342,645 sentences) and the professor dataset contains 283,973 texts (976,677 sentences).
They used a random baseline (20% for the Word Intrusion task) and the centroid as a baseline label for cluster labeling.
They tend to rank low, often producing flirtatious or retaliatory responses and rarely politely refuse.
They collected 9960 HITs from 472 crowd workers.
Yes; the customised HeidelTime achieved an F1 value of 89.1 for partial identification and normalization.
agglutinative and fusional

Yes — the study treats and analyzes both derivational and inflectional morphology: Turkish data include derivational boundaries and inflectional groups, and analyses separately evaluate sentences with derived words and inflectional/contextual ambiguity.
The lemma of the token followed by language-specific morphological tags (with additional information such as parts-of-speech for some languages, e.g., Turkish).

The Semantic Scholar corpus (SemScholar), Springer Nature’s SciGraph, the Textbook Question Answering (TQA) corpus, the January 2018 English Wikipedia dataset, and the Flickr30K and COCO image-sentence matching datasets.

The training, validation and test datasets provided for the shared task (Mohammad and Bravo-Marquez, 2017a), consisting of tweets annotated for four emotions: joy, sadness, fear, and anger.

English, French, and Arabic.

They consider (a) whether the text is direct or indirect; (b) whether it is offensive, disrespectful, hateful, fearful out of ignorance, abusive, or normal; (c) the attribute based on which it discriminates against an individual or a group; (d) the name of this group; and (e) how the annotators feel about its content within a range of negative to neutral sentiments.
The final dataset contains a pilot corpus of 100 tweets per language and comparable corpora of 5,647 English tweets, 4,014 French tweets, and 3,353 Arabic tweets.

Semantic Web technologies can be used to improve semantic disambiguation/WSD by linking words to resources (e.g., DBpedia) and using topic modelling plus SPARQL to choose translations; support syntactic disambiguation and tense/structure issues via ontology relationships and reasoning; enhance named-entity recognition and disambiguation by linking entities to KBs and encoding entity types into MT training; handle non-standard speech and idioms by using user characteristics and linked idiom knowledge graphs; and translate and enrich knowledge bases (e.g., DBpedia) via bilingual embeddings and neural models to supply external resources for MT.

Challenges include that SWT-based pre- and post-editing disambiguation techniques are tedious to implement for common words and often must be applied multiple times, and the use of two or more SW resources simultaneously has not yet been investigated. There is also a lack of good solutions for syntactic disambiguation and a well-known poor run-time performance of reasoners that must be addressed before integrating SWT into MT systems.

Structural divergence; linguistic properties/features (complex tense systems, grammatical gender and morphology); excessive focus on English and European languages / poor research on low-resource language pairs; limitations of SMT across domains; non-standard speech (social media slang, idioms, MWEs, abbreviations); difficult translations among morphologically rich languages; and mismatch between parallel training data and real user speech for speech translation.
The EEG features used were root mean square, zero crossing rate, moving window average, kurtosis and power spectral entropy.

USC-TIMIT database.

Yes; they evaluate weight pruning, matrix factorization, and hybrid pruning and find the hybrid (matrix factorization + weight pruning) combination yields better gains than single-method solutions.
SST-2, QQP, MNLI-m, MNLI-mm, and QNLI.

Yes — they include and compare to a neural abstractive baseline (NN-ABS) in both ROUGE and human evaluations; NN-WE consistently outperforms NN-ABS, and NN-SE is significantly better than NN-ABS in human evaluation.

They focus on ontologies of hierarchical data expressed as transitive entailment (IS‑A) relations, including complex ordering structure, compositional multi‑word entities, and commonsense knowledge mined from large‑scale unstructured text.

They mean leveraging the geometric properties of learned representation spaces to make predictions that automatically obey complex structural constraints, such as hierarchical and partially-ordered structures.

Yes — they used sentiment pre-training, including a bilateral LSTM trained on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank and pretrained sentiment classifiers such as the OpenAI model trained on 82 million Amazon reviews (and the TBSA model).

The models extracted n-grams (word unigrams and bigrams transformed using TF and IDF), distributed semantics (300-dimensional word2vec embeddings averaged per segment, with experiments using GloVe and Sent2Vec), and sentiment features (word percentages across 192 Empath lexical categories and pre-trained sentiment embeddings learned with a bilateral LSTM trained on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank).

text, graphics, arrows and arrowheads
Crowd-sourced annotators were non-expert annotators on Amazon Mechanical Turk. Expert annotations were created by trained experts for AI2D-RST.
The results show that the identity of diagram elements can be learned from their layout features, while the expert annotations provide better representations of diagram types.
Amazon Mechanical Turk
Trained experts are the annotators who created the AI2D-RST annotations for a subset of 1000 diagrams (five annotators who applied the multi-layer annotation schema).
They opt for Recurrent Neural Networks (Hierarchical Attention Network, HAN) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN).

They use the Clueweb09-derived dataset of [4] for baseline comparison and a newly generated Wikipedia-derived dataset crawled from Wikipedia's 'List of Controversial articles' (2009 and 2018) for cross-temporal, cross-topic and cross-domain experiments.
Cross-domain

They use weak-signal data derived from a Wikipedia crawl seeded from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” (2009 and 2018), collecting pages up to two hops via “See also”, “References” and “External links” and assuming linked pages inherit controversial labels, with non-controversial seeds from Wikipedia’s random article endpoint.
Yes — they compare their semantic (neural embedding) approaches to lexical baselines including TfIdf‑SVM and language models (LM, LM‑DBPedia), as well as matching and sentiment baselines.
About 64M segments from YouTube videos comprising a total of 1.2B tokens.
About 64 M segments comprising a total of 1.2 B tokens.
1500-dimensional frame-level visual features extracted from video frames at 1-second intervals.

Yes — the LSTM-based NMT architecture used in the experiments implements the attention mechanism (the "scaled Luong" version). They also used a default OpenNMT model (which used the "scaled Luong" attention) and experimented with a Transformer model.
Neural machine translation (NMT) models and the Transformer model (Vaswani et al., 2017).

Symbolic rewriting is the transformation of symbolic expressions according to rewrite rules (e.g., replacing terms using equalities as in the AIM dataset) to produce equivalent or normalized forms (as in the polynomial dataset where arbitrary polynomials are converted to a normalized form).

They allow experts to specify anchor variables (anchor words) R and add a mutual-information term β Σ_{(i,j)∈R} I(X_i; Y_j) to the CorEx objective so latent factors are seeded with and preserve information about those expert-specified anchors (e.g., anchoring a “diabetes” factor to the word “diabetes”).
They evaluate on the 20 Newsgroups and the i2b2 Obesity Challenge 2008 corpora.
No; they discuss SeededLDA in Related Work but do not perform empirical comparisons to LDA—experiments compare Anchored CorEx to Unsupervised CorEx and Naive Bayes.
Peng and Dredze (2016) is the state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese Social media.
Sina Weibo.
They used a modified labelled Weibo NER corpus (as in Peng and Dredze (2016)). They also used the same unlabeled Sina Weibo text (112,971,734 Weibo messages).
XNLI (Cross-lingual NLI); MLDoc (Document Classification); Cross-lingual Intent Classification; POS Tagging; Named Entity Recognition.

Yes — the mNMT/MMTE representations yield larger gains on low-resource languages, improving over bilingual baselines and outperforming mBERT particularly on low-resource languages, while the mNMT model performs worse than bilingual baselines on high-resource language pairs.

They do both: in the in-language setting they train one model for each language (i.e., fine-tune per language), and they also run a "one model for all languages" experiment where they concatenate all data and train a single model jointly on all languages.
These languages are chosen based on intersection of languages for which POS labels are available in the Universal Dependencies dataset and the languages supported by our mNMT model.
They use both automatic and human evaluation.
They lag by a significant gap.

They use a translation-based Poly-encoder (Poly) that uses the Google Translate API to translate target queries to English and translate English responses back to the target language (target query → English query → English response → target response).
Chinese, French, Indonesian, Italian, Korean, and Japanese.
Yes; they automatically created a test set of contrastive examples from the OpenSubtitles corpus.

Recurrent baseline: a standard bidirectional RNN model with attention, trained with Nematus, operating on the sentence level and not seeing any additional context; Transformer baseline: a standard context-agnostic Transformer with all model parameters identical to a Transformer-base (Vaswani et al., 2017).
Recurrent models: concat22, s-hier, s-t-hier, s-hier-to-2 (with weight-tying variants); Transformer models: concat22, concat21, and the Voita et al. (2018) context-aware Transformer.
English → German

They use a simple EL algorithm that links each mention to the entity with the highest commonness score, where commonness is computed from Wikipedia anchor links estimating the probability of an entity given the mention string. The commonness score is used as the confidence g in prediction, and within a document they apply the Ganea and Hofmann (2017) heuristic to coreference generic person mentions to more specific mentions.

A two-layer BiLSTM for context encoding combined with mention-string and KB-type representations, concatenated and fed into a three-layer MLP whose output is scored via dot products with embedded type vectors.
FIGER (GOLD) and BBN.

Precision, recall, F1-score, support, accuracy, and accuracy without the ‘O’-tag.
The model gives an F1-score of 0.89 for the concept recognition task.
Yes. Implementation with demo and dataset is available at: https://github.com/jitinkrishnan/NASA-SE.

The BIO labelling scheme uses the following labels: abb (abbreviations such as TRL), grp (groups of people or individuals such as Electrical Engineers, Systems Engineers, Project Manager), syscon (system concepts such as engineering unit, product, hardware, software), opcon (operational concepts such as decision analysis process, technology maturity assessment, system requirements review), seterm (generic SE terms such as project, mission, key performance parameter, audit), event (event-like information such as Pre-Phase A, Phase A, Phase B), org (organizations such as NASA, aerospace industry), art (artifact or instrument names such as AS1300), cardinal (numerical values such as 1, 100, one), loc (location-like entities such as component facilities or centralized facility), and mea (measures, features, or behaviors such as cost, risk, feasibility).
BERT
Roughly 3700 sentences.
precision, recall and F-measure.
The position of the sentence, sentence length and tense.
They used classical word2vec word embeddings (averaged to form sentence vectors), paragraph vectors (PV-DM), and BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE) trained in a sentiment-specific style.
No — they use the Argumentative Zoning Corpus (AZ corpus), consisting of 80 AZ-annotated conference articles drawn from the Cmplg arXiv, yielding 7,347 labeled sentences.

Sentence embeddings are learned from a large text corpus using three methods: (1) averaging word2vec word embeddings for the words in a sentence (V_avgwvec(w) = (1/n) sum_i W^w_{x_i}), (2) training paragraph vectors (PV-DM) which learns paragraph vectors in matrix D jointly with word matrix W, and (3) training BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE) and then averaging those word vectors per sentence.
Argumentative zoning (AZ) is rhetorical sentence classification, a process of assigning rhetorical status to the extracted sentences.

They crawled two credit-based freemium blackmarket sites (YouLikeHits and Like4Like) from February to April 2019, created dummy accounts (with IRB approval) to participate and recorded the Tweet IDs of tweets posted to gain retweets. They then used Twitter’s REST API to collect the tweet objects and also collected the timelines of the authors to find genuine tweets not posted to the blackmarket sites.
State-of-the-art Twitter spam detection methods.
English
They look at freemium blackmarket services: social-share services, credit-based services, and auto-time retweet services.
They pretrain two-language (English/Chinese) and three-language (English/French/Chinese) XNLG models and initialize from a 15-language pre-trained XLM.
A 6-layer Transformer decoder with 1024 hidden units per layer, 8 attention heads, and GELU activations.

They compare against: (1) Language Models — gated convolutional language model (GCNN) with and without added self-attention; (2) seq2seq — LSTMs and convolutional seq2seq architectures, including Conv seq2seq with decoder self-attention; (3) Ensemble — an ensemble of two Conv seq2seq with self-attention models; and (4) KNN — a nearest-neighbor retrieval model using FastText/TF-IDF vectors and FAISS.
No — they pretrain a seq2seq model on the Writing Prompts dataset and use learned embeddings (embedding size 300 for the GCNN model and 256 for the Conv seq2seq).

They explore CountVectorizer, TF‑IDFVectorizer, and Word2Vec for unsupervised pre‑training, and fine‑tune Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Long Short Term Memory networks (LSTMs), Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machine (SVM).
They extracted six groups of features: Numerical features, Language models, Word Clusters, Topic Models, Part-of-Speech tags, and Essay text (bigram bag-of-words with idf weighting).

Further analysis and validation to investigate whether LDA topic distributions reflect true modeling power or are an artifact of the dataset, which requires the topics of the essays to be released so validation splits can be done on the basis of these topics.
A tuned Logistic Regression with the same class weights for the class imbalance.

Manually, using a 5-point scale (as in Kocabiyikoglu et al. (2018)) for text-text alignment: 1 = Wrong alignment, 2 = Partial alignment with slightly compositional translational equivalence, 3 = Partial alignment with compositional translation and additional or missing information, 4 = Correct alignment with compositional translation and few additional or missing information, 5 = Correct alignment and fully compositional translation; samples were split into three bins by hunalign confidence and two annotators each rated 30 items per bin (with 10 overlapping to calculate inter-annotator reliability).

The audio-text alignment quality was evaluated manually using a 3-point scale: 1 Wrong alignment; 2 Partial alignment, some words or sentences may be missing; 3 Correct alignment, allowing non-spoken syllables at start or end. The experiment was performed by two annotators who each rated 30 items from each hunalign-confidence bin, with 10 overlapping items to calculate inter-annotator reliability.

Yes. The selective gate is specifically aimed to handle one-sentence bags by directly and dynamically assigning low gating values to wrongly labeled instances, preventing noisy representations from being propagated.

The approach is based on the assumption that enhancing frequencies of related words or phrases (when they co-occur) increases their mutual contribution to topics, but a direct implementation with WordNet relations degraded the unigram model; correcting the assumptions (excluding hyponyms) and adding ngrams improved the model, and using a domain-specific thesaurus (EuroVoc) improved the model without extra assumptions.
Economic and political domains.
They used WordNet, EuroVoc, and RuThes.

Drawing upon these definitions, we define hate speech as language that is used to expresses hatred towards a targeted group or is intended to be derogatory, to humiliate, or to insult the members of the group. In extreme cases this may also be language that threatens or incites violence, but limiting our definition only to such cases would exclude a large proportion of hate speech.
They train a logistic regression model with L2 regularization.

By estimating P(y|h) via K-fold cross-prediction and assigning each training sample a weight w = 1/((1−ε)P(y|h)+ε) (with normalization of label-weight sums), the framework creates an artifact-balanced distribution that balances artifacts across labels and thus mitigates the bias pattern.

The model did not have flexibility in OOV words and for unknown words it assigned a zero vector, which affected testing results and caused a large drop in performance (OOV test F1 72.22 vs 96.24). The authors note this lack of flexibility could also mean the model recognized a mismatch of a wrong word with its class, and they mitigated the issue by obtaining subword vectors from FastText for unknown words.

The dataset is a sample of 10,417 users retrieved from a database containing more than 400 million publicly available tweets posted by more than 100,000 users who have announced their pregnancy; two professional annotators manually categorized users as "bot," "non-bot," or "unavailable" based on their publicly available Twitter sites, labeling "bot" if they did not appear to be posting personal information and "unavailable" if their Twitter sites could not be viewed. Inter-annotator agreement on 1,000 overlapping annotations was κ = 0.93, and after resolving disagreements 413 (4%) users were annotated as "bot," 7,849 (75.35%) as "non-bot," and 20 . 69 (19 . 9%) as "unavailable".

By extending an existing system (Botometer) with additional health-related features and using a statistical machine-learning classifier (gradient boosting) that uses the Botometer score as a feature, while addressing class imbalance with SMOTE and tuning parameters via cross-validation. The added features include tweet diversity, URL score, mean daily posts, topic heterogeneity via LDA, mean post length, profile-picture face detection, and detection of a person’s name in the user name.
Adverse drug reaction detection, syndromic surveillance, subject recruitment for cancer trials, and characterizing drug abuse.

The new dataset, PICONET, was created by collecting 363,078 structured abstracts from PubMed (searched April 11, 2019) using filters for Article Type = Clinical Trial, Species = Humans, and Language = English, and then choosing abstract section headings representative of Population, Intervention, and Outcome by lemmatizing section labels and manually inspecting samples. They avoided a keyword-based approach, kept full abstract sections (using multi-labels for some headings), included negative examples from headings like "aim", and cleaned the data (language identification, removal of missing values, unicode cleaning) and filtered sequences to 5–200 words.

It identifies relationships between elements of a sentence and chunks of words using POS tags and a dependency-based parse tree: main components (subject, verb, object) are recognized by program Π2 with rules based on Universal Dependency relations (with special handling for structure #4). Complement components are then identified by program Π3, which encodes non-core and nominal dependents and is called recursively to find maximal chunks supporting the main components, producing a list of vocabularies for the next steps.

Yes — they hypothesize that MagiCoder’s pure syntactic word-by-word matching together with restrictive selection criteria (e.g., only completely covered LLTs and coverage-density favoring nearby words), plus stemming and synonymy issues, make it favor short, compact descriptions while longer descriptions yield partially covered terms, more synonyms/noise and thus lower performance.

The baseline is a one-stage RNN system whose RNN is the same as the last stage (2-layer BLSTM) and is directly trained to recognize dialect category.

The two-stage system achieves 88.88% overall accuracy (87.72% ≤3s, 90.04% >3s), improving by about 10% relative to the baseline (78.85% overall, 77.60% ≤3s, 80.10% >3s), while the three-stage system achieves 87.24% overall (85.52% ≤3s, 88.96% >3s).

The restricted track organizers provided 1.2M parallel pairs (560K without identity mappings) of corrected and uncorrected sentences, and the authors' restricted-track system was a purely neural system that did not use any external NLP tools, spell checkers, or hand-crafted confusion sets.
Building Educational Applications
There is no clear winner: MODULAR is better at reducing bias and has higher accuracy, while CONCURRENT produces more fluent responses and preserves meaning better.
They were made by English Wikipedia editors (i.e., a Wiki community effort).

By treating neutralization as a translation task that maps a subjective source sentence s to a neutral target t, the paper proposes two algorithms: MODULAR and CONCURRENT. MODULAR first uses a BERT-based detection module to tag subjective words and an LSTM editing module (with copy, coverage, a pre-trained neutral decoder, and a join embedding that gates detector outputs into encoder hidden states) to edit the sentence, while CONCURRENT directly generates a neutralized sentence with a BERT encoder and attentional LSTM decoder (also using copy, coverage and autoencoding pretraining); both are fine-tuned on WNC with a token-weighted loss.
Investigating the suitability of skeletal data for recognizing ASL signs.
The best model, Spatial AI-LSTM, achieves 81% average cross-subject accuracy with a 6% standard deviation.
